summary: So Many Roads shines, New Speedway Boogie roars amid uneven set
blurb: The show delivers a soaring So Many Roads and an electrifying New Speedway Boogie, with Jerry’s vocal power front‑and‑center. Stagger Lee and the Big Railroad > Memphis Blues > Boogie sequence also stand out, though the Help‑Slip‑Franklin transition falters. Overall the set is uneven but offers several high‑water moments.
review: On June 23, 1992 at Star Lake Amphitheater, the Dead gave a night that swings between brilliance and wobble. Jerry Garcia’s voice soars on “So Many Roads” and his raw howl powers “New Speedway Boogie,” while his solo on “Stagger Lee” earns high praise. The first set is charged with energy – “Help On The Way → Slipknot → Franklin's Tower” attempts a bold segue, but many listeners note timing slips and thin synth support from Vince. The second half recovers with the gritty “Big Railroad Blues → Stuck Inside of Mobile with the Memphis Blues Again → New Speedway Boogie” medley and a heartfelt “The Last Time,” capped by a sweet “Sugar Magnolia.” Phil Lesh’s bass remains a steady backbone throughout, and both Mickey Hart and Bill Kreutzmann keep the groove rock‑solid. Vince Welnick’s keyboards are uneven, sounding thin on the Help/Slip transition but otherwise staying out of the way. In short, the concert is a mixed bag: unforgettable moments sit beside some lackluster passages, making it worth a spin for fans who love the high points.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Across four recordings the sentiment is mixed: multiple reviewers applaud "So Many Roads," "New Speedway Boogie," and strong solos, but they also flag uneven pacing, a sloppy Help‑Slip‑Franklin transition, and thin keyboards. The balance of positive highlights versus consistent criticisms yields a modest positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Good‑quality soundboard capture with clear mix; highlights So Many Roads and New Speedway Boogie while minimizing tape hiss.

key_highlights: So Many Roads – soaring vocal and emotive guitar work
New Speedway Boogie – explosive energy and powerful vocals
Big Railroad Blues → Stuck Inside of Mobile with the Memphis Blues Again → New Speedway Boogie medley
The Last Time and Sugar Magnolia provide strong second‑set peaks despite overall unevenness
song_highlights: Stuck Inside of Mobile with the Memphis Blues Again

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers soaring vocals on So Many Roads and a raw howl on New Speedway Boogie; his Stagger Lee solo shines, but early timing slips and the Help‑Slip transition are criticized.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh provides a solid, steady bass foundation throughout, praised as the show’s rhythmic backbone.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s rhythm guitar stays in service of the songs; his vocals rise prominently on The Last Time, though he isn’t a focal point of most reviews.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart’s drumming is noted as spot‑on, especially during the post‑drums > space sequence that some reviewers called spectacular.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann keeps a tight groove; both drummers are highlighted as steady and “spot on” in multiple analyses.

must_listen_sequences: Stuck Inside of Mobile with the Memphis Blues Again
